title: Hogarth, Marriage a la Mode
description: Test your knowledge on this object.
topic_path: British art in the eighteenth century

assessment_items.question_content:
**The popularity of Hogarth's *Marriage å la Mode* reflects what cultural change in 18th century England? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Critical of the wealthy aristocracy, Hogarth's series found great popularity among England's growing middle class.

choices.content: the growth of middle class audiences for art
the rise of a powerful aristocracy
the reliance on an agrarian economy
the rejection of traditional moral values

assessment_items.question_content:
**The second in the *Marriage å la Mode* series, what is the title of this image?**

![Interior scene of a wealthy couple in disarray, with the husband slouched as a dog tugs at his pocket, the wife yawning, and a servant holding bills.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/08222da4d9f2aa8dd73e424bbbe7ab7e.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Marriage Settlement, is the first of the series, introduces the story about the wealthy young Squanderfield and his new bride.
The term "toilette" refers to the process of grooming and dressing in preparation for an event.
This scene, *The Tête à Tête,* shows an early morning encounter between the couple as evidence of their mutual infidelity.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the theme of Hogarth's *Marriage å la Mode* series?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The story revolves around an arranged marriage between the daughter of a wealthy merchant and the aristocrat Squanderfield.

Together the images provide a moral tale warning against the social practice of marrying for money and social status instead of for love.  Hogarth used satire to indict the upper classes for their hypocrisy, indulgent lifestyle, and greed.

choices.content: It's a moral indictment of hypocrisy, indulgence and greed among the upper class
It's a political critique of the aristocracy's blind faith in the King.
It's a motivational statement directing the middle class to aspire to aristocratic goals.
It's a cautionary message warning of human mortality and the importance of a spiritual life.
It's an idealized narrative representing everyday life in a modern British home.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which details in Hogarth's painting allude to the moral decline of the characters? **

![Interior scene of a wealthy couple in disarray, with the husband slouched as a dog tugs at his pocket, the wife yawning, and a servant holding bills.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/08222da4d9f2aa8dd73e424bbbe7ab7e.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Hogarth includes uses art to highlight the decline of traditional ideals in this family's home.
The black spot on the man's neck is a visible sign of syphilis and indicates the man's infidelity.
All of the details above indicate the moral decline of the couple.

choices.content: the paintings in the far room
the musical instruments on the floor
the black spot on the man's neck

assessment_items.question_content:
**Place the events listed in the left-hand column in the proper sequence, then match the images in the right-hand column to 
the corresponding events.**  

[[☃ matcher 1]]
hints:
The correct sequence of the series is as follows: 

1 The Marriage Settlement (The contract is signed.)
![Interior scene with a wealthy couple and companions surrounded by paintings, as lawyers examine documents and others interact with pets, suggesting disorder and corruption.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b23580917250641e842995f26beb3183.jpg) 

2 The Tête à Tête (The couple meets the morning after a night of debauchery.)
 ![Interior scene of a wealthy couple in disarray, with the husband slouched as a dog tugs at his pocket, the wife yawning, and a servant holding bills.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c8373e116a91b12273f1decbe06c57ea.jpg)

3 The Inspection (Squanderfield has infected a girl with syphilis and confronts the doctor.)

 ![Interior scene with a man holding a sword confronting another man, while a woman begs and others look on in a chaotic room.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7fb9705111c64f488dfd5e528c3d74b2.jpg)

4 The Toilette (The wife entertains in her chambers.)

![Interior scene with a man holding a sword confronting another man, while a woman begs and others look on in a chaotic room.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a2e4e169433a66cffa6c6fafb6d15e26.jpg) 

5 The Bagnio (After the lovers are caught, Silvertongue stabs Squanderfield.)

![Interior scene with a wife kneeling and pleading, a wounded husband staggering back, and a servant rummaging through a chest in a disordered room.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/09310f04868e5be63fd7f09da703cc59.jpg) 

6 The Lady's Death (After Silvertongue is hanged for murder, the woman drinks poison.)

![Interior scene with a woman collapsing as attendants rush to help, while three men look on, set in a richly decorated room with a stained glass window.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ab0258baa39074b658e0c589814b8e27.jpg)

left: The marriage is arranged and the contract signed.
Squanderfield's lifestyle is shown to have infected others.
The lovers are caught and Squanderfied is stabbed.
The wife drinks poison after the death of her lover.
right: ![Interior scene with a wealthy couple and companions surrounded by paintings, as lawyers examine documents and others interact with pets, suggesting disorder and corruption.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b23580917250641e842995f26beb3183.jpg)

![Interior scene of a wealthy couple in disarray, with the husband slouched as a dog tugs at his pocket, the wife yawning, and a servant holding bills.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c8373e116a91b12273f1decbe06c57ea.jpg)
![Interior scene with a man holding a sword confronting another man, while a woman begs and others look on in a chaotic room.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7fb9705111c64f488dfd5e528c3d74b2.jpg)
![Lavish interior scene with elegantly dressed figures drinking, flirting, and gambling, while servants and animals add to the disorder.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a2e4e169433a66cffa6c6fafb6d15e26.jpg)

![Interior scene with a wife kneeling and pleading, a wounded husband staggering back, and a servant rummaging through a chest in a disordered room.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/09310f04868e5be63fd7f09da703cc59.jpg)

![Interior scene with a woman collapsing as attendants rush to help, while three men look on, set in a richly decorated room with a stained glass window.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ab0258baa39074b658e0c589814b8e27.jpg)

assessment_items.question_content:
**What literary device did Hogarth adapt in *Marriage å la Mode* ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Like many authors of the time, Hogarth used irony and satire to offer a critique of contemporary culture.
